Simulation notes for the auxiliary-weighted CP experiments

Full run with max_permutations = 500. I wrote this as a quick reading guide for the current simulation output.

Run setting. I used state space {1,2,3}, dense adjacency, n_sim = 500, T = 500, horizons h = 1, 2, 3, alpha = 0.05, 0.10, ..., 0.50, and randomized_ties = False. The methods compared are original i-block CP, the D!-weighted auxiliary method, and the D-weighted auxiliary method. The value max_permutations = 500 is only the cap on sampled block permutations used when estimating each p-value. It does not change D or D!.

Files used. The numbers below come from simulation_results/all_summary.csv and the four per-case summaries. The raw replicate-level outputs are in sim*_raw_results.csv.

My current reading

  • For the ordinary dense stochastic case, the D-weighted method looks like a reasonable compromise: smaller sets than original CP, but less aggressive than D!-weighting.
  • The deterministic examples are useful because they expose the empty-set problem for the D!-weighted rule.
  • The single-state example is encouraging for the Sweden-like case. Original CP covers but keeps many paths, while the weighted versions return the natural all-1 path.
  • I would not claim from these plots that the weighted q is a valid p-value. The simulations only show that the empirical behavior is promising in these examples.
  • My current view is that D-weighting is a better computational comparison than D!-weighting, but the validity question still needs a separate argument.

Sim 1: Dense three-state Markov chain

  • This is the main non-deterministic dense Markov-chain case. All three states can appear with positive probability.
  • My reading is that the original i-block method is still the most conservative method here. It usually gives the largest sets.
  • The D!-weighted version gives smaller sets, but for h = 2 and h = 3 it drops below the target coverage at alpha = 0.20.
  • The D-weighted version sits between those two. It reduces the set size compared with original CP, while staying closer to the desired coverage.

Alpha = 0.20

MethodhCoverageMCSEMean set sizeScaled size
Original i-block CP10.9060.0132.4240.808
Permutation-count (D!) weighted10.8000.0181.8640.621

Permutation-count (D!) weighted30.7460.01914.1220.523
I-block-count (D) weighted30.8140.01716.5260.612

Sim 2: Two-cycle example

  • This is the deterministic two-cycle case, so it is more of a stress test than a realistic stochastic example.
  • Original CP covers the true future, but it keeps extra paths in the set.
  • The D!-weighted version is too aggressive in this example. At alpha = 0.20 it gives empty sets for h = 1 and h = 3.
  • The D-weighted version gives the singleton path that matches the deterministic future for h = 1, 2, and 3.

Alpha = 0.20

Sim 3: Three-cycle example

  • This is the deterministic three-cycle case.
  • Original CP already does very well here. At alpha = 0.20 it gives the correct singleton set for all three horizons.
  • The D-weighted version matches that behavior at alpha = 0.20.
  • The D!-weighted version again has the empty-set issue for longer horizons.

Alpha = 0.20

Sim 4: Single-state example, like the Sweden case

  • This is the extra case Prof. Kendall asked about: the process starts at state 1 and stays at state 1, so the observed history contains only state 1.
  • All three methods cover the true future path, but original CP is very wide.
  • At alpha = 0.20, both weighted auxiliary versions reduce the final prediction set to the all-1 singleton path for h = 1, 2, and 3.
  • I think this is the most direct simulation check for the Sweden-like situation, but I would still treat it as empirical evidence rather than a validity argument.

Alpha = 0.20
